prompt: Which ancient wonder was still standing in the medieval era and survives to this day?
Julius Caesar was famously assassinated on the 'Ides of March' — what date is that?
What did Romans use instead of toilet paper? (They shared one across a public bathroom.)
The Great Wall of China was primarily built to defend against which group?
Napoleon was actually average height for his era (around 5'7"). Why did people think he was tiny?
options: He wore flat shoes while other officers wore heeled boots
British political cartoons exaggerated his size for propaganda
He always stood next to very tall bodyguards
His enemies bribed historians to record him as short

prompt: The Black Death killed roughly what fraction of Europe's population in the 14th century?
Which US president was the first to appear on television — broadcasting from the 1939 World's Fair?
Ancient Egyptian pyramid builders weren't slaves — they were paid workers. What was a key part of their daily wage?
The Library of Alexandria declined over centuries rather than one dramatic fire. Which event is considered the earliest major blow to its holdings?
options: Julius Caesar accidentally burned part of it during the Alexandrian War in 48 BC
The Arab conquest of Egypt in 642 AD
Emperor Theodosius ordering pagan temples closed in 391 AD

prompt: The Byzantine Empire called itself 'Roman' until its fall in 1453. What did its citizens typically call themselves?
The Mongol Empire was the largest contiguous land empire in history. At its peak, roughly what share of the world's land did it cover?
Which ship famously sank on its maiden voyage in 1912 after hitting an iceberg?
Cleopatra lived closer in time to which of these than to the building of the Great Pyramid?
Who was the nurse known as 'The Lady with the Lamp' during the Crimean War?
Vikings reached North America around 1000 AD. What name did they give the land they found?
Which wall, dividing a European city for decades, fell in 1989?
The last woolly mammoths died out on a remote Arctic island surprisingly recently — by which point one of these was already built?
options: Stonehenge had not yet been started

prompt: Which document, signed by King John of England in 1215, limited the power of the monarchy?
Roman soldiers were sometimes partly paid in a valuable commodity, which is said to be the origin of the word 'salary'. What was it?
The Aztec capital Tenochtitlan was built on an island in the middle of a lake. What city stands over its ruins today?
How did the Inca Empire keep detailed records and numbers without a writing system?
On his 1324 pilgrimage, Mansa Musa of Mali handed out so much of one thing in Cairo that he wrecked its value there for years. What was it?
Despite its name, the Hundred Years' War between England and France actually lasted how long?
Which English king had six wives and broke with the Catholic Church partly to divorce the first of them?
The Rosetta Stone unlocked Egyptian hieroglyphs because it repeated one text in three scripts. Which language on it was the key scholars could already read?
In 1518 in Strasbourg, hundreds of people were reportedly struck by an outbreak of what bizarre, sometimes deadly behavior?
The Treaty of Tordesillas (1494) drew a line dividing newly 'discovered' lands between which two countries?
The shortest recorded war in history, between Britain and Zanzibar in 1896, lasted roughly how long?
Emperor Qin Shi Huang of China was buried with an army of thousands of life-sized figures. What were they made of?
Which ancient civilization built elaborate covered drains and flushing-style toilets in cities like Mohenjo-daro over 4,000 years ago?
The ancient Olympic Games were held in honour of which Greek god?
Which Roman city was buried and preserved when Mount Vesuvius erupted in 79 AD?
American colonists protested a tax by dumping crates of what into a harbour in 1773?
Which explorer's crew completed the first voyage all the way around the world, though he himself died partway?
The Great Sphinx of Giza is famously missing which feature?
According to Roman legend, the city of Rome was founded by twin brothers raised by what animal?
The famous tomb of which young Egyptian pharaoh was found almost intact in 1922?
In 1630s Holland, a speculative bubble saw single bulbs of what flower trade for the price of a house?
When the mummy of Ramesses II was flown to France in 1976 for restoration, what did officials issue so it could legally enter the country?
options: An Egyptian passport listing his occupation as 'King (deceased)'

prompt: The Thirty Years' War was partly sparked in 1618 when angry officials threw imperial envoys out of a castle window in which event?
Which empire conquered Constantinople in 1453, ending the Byzantine Empire?
Eruptions of Mount Tambora in 1815 caused such global cooling that 1816 became known as what?
Which gladiator led a major slave uprising against the Roman Republic around 73 BC?
Johannes Gutenberg's mid-1400s printing press is famous for mass-producing which book first?
In 1932, Australia deployed soldiers with machine guns against a destructive bird population in what became mockingly known as the 'Emu War'. Who is generally said to have won?
options: It ended in a signed truce
Neither — it was called off before any contact

prompt: A corroded bronze device recovered from a Roman-era shipwreck near Antikythera turned out to be an ancient analog computer for tracking what?
Modern engineers have found that Roman concrete can heal its own cracks over time, thanks largely to its use of what ingredient?
At the Battle of Cajamarca in 1532, a small band of Spanish conquistadors under Francisco Pizarro captured which ruler?
Genghis Khan unified the Mongol tribes, but 'Genghis Khan' was a title he was given. What was his birth name?
